title: How Does a Vision Sequence Work?
description:
How Does a Vision Sequence Work?

How does a vision sequence work?

Vision sequences contain one or more vision objects and the fundamental purpose of the vision sequence is to execute these vision objects in sequential order (as previously defined by the user.)  The following steps show what happens when a vision sequence executes:

If the Vision Sequence was initiated from the VRUN SPEL language command, then control is passed back to SPEL immediately after the VRUN command is acknowledged (before any vision processing occurs.)  This allows other things to be done in SPEL while Vision Guide is doing the Vision processing.

IMPORTANT NOTE: It is important to understand that the VRun command may return control back to SPEL before the image has been acquired or during the middle of image acquisition. In most cases this is not a problem. However, if you have a motion statement immediately following the VRUN statement in your SPEL program, pay close attention to the image (maybe by testing it using the RuntimeFreeze property set to true) to make sure it is captured when the robot is stopped and not during the beginning of the next motion. You may want to insert a WAIT statement following the VRUN statement if this becomes a problem.

The Vision Sequence uses it's property settings to set up the execution of the sequence.  These include things like which camera to use to acquire an image, determining the method to use to acquire an image (Strobed, Stationary, or No acquire at all).
The image is acquired and placed in the frame buffer for use by the vision objects which will execute next. (If the RuntimeAcquire property is set to "None", then the image which was already in the frame buffer is used for the sequence.  This allows multiple sequences to operate on the same image.)
Vision objects are now executed in sequential order using the image acquired in step 2 above.  Once all the vision objects have been executed, Sequence and object results are available for display on the Sequence and Object windows and are also available from the SPEL Language .
